policy_statement: Establish an Offsite GHG Reduction Program for new development to use as a GHG reduction/mitigation pathway for CAP compliance and to fund programs reducing GHG emissions in the built environment.
parent_statement: Strategy 1: Decarbonize the Energy Supply through measures including a sunset strategy for oil and gas operations, procuring zero-carbon electricity, increasing renewable energy production, increasing energy resilience, and establishing GHG requirements for new development.
verbatim_text: Action ES5.4 - Establish an Offsite GHG Reduction Program for new development to use as a GHG reduction or mitigation pathway for 2045 CAP compliance and to fund programs for reducing GHG emissions in the built environment.
extraction_rationale: Creates a defined program mechanism for mitigation/compliance and funding.
mechanism_description: Establishes an offsite GHG reduction program for new development that provides a mitigation pathway for CAP compliance and funds programs that reduce GHG emissions in the built environment.
primary_causal_pathway: Policy mechanism (fees/credits/funding) → Offsite implementation of emissions-reduction projects → ↓ energy/fuel use or ↓ high-GWP refrigerant leakage → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: New development is set or enabled to comply with climate targets by paying into or using an offsite GHG reduction program (e.g., purchasing credits, paying an in-lieu fee, or directly funding eligible projects) rather than—or in addition to—onsite measures.  
2) Mediating physical process: Program-funded projects (commonly building energy retrofits, electrification, renewable energy procurement, efficiency upgrades, or refrigerant management) reduce fossil fuel combustion and/or reduce leakage of high-global-warming-potential gases, thereby lowering CO₂e.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in community/built-environment GHG emissions relative to a baseline, helping meet CAP targets and lowering cumulative emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing net GHG emissions by funding and/or procuring offsite emissions-reduction measures associated with new development.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s primary intent and effect is to achieve quantified GHG reductions via offsite actions, leading to lower net emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the mechanism is implemented through governance/finance tools and can improve energy efficiency (a resource-efficiency co-benefit), the defining outcome is emissions reduction (mitigation) rather than “same service with less input” as the endpoint.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an offsite program were to fund adaptation-only projects (e.g., cooling centers) without material GHG impacts, it would no longer fit this canonical mechanism; under the stated mechanism (offsite GHG reduction), mitigation remains dominant.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that establishes an offsite GHG reduction/mitigation pathway for development compliance and funds emissions-reducing built-environment programs has mitigation as its primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the funded measures are efficiency, electrification, renewables, refrigerants, or offsets/credits is policy-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level based on evidence and MRV rules.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Key edge cases are MRV/additionality (true reductions vs. accounting), whether credits are local vs. external, and the possibility of funding measures with weak or indirect GHG impacts; these affect effectiveness but not the mechanism’s primary classification as mitigation.
key_indicators: "Establish an Offsite GHG Reduction Program"; "for new development"; "GHG reduction or mitigation pathway"; "CAP compliance"; "fund programs for reducing GHG emissions in the built environment"
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements from reduced combustion; potential energy cost savings from efficiency/electrification (not explicitly stated)
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented as a regulatory permit condition (mandate) requiring payment/participation; however the text emphasizes establishing a program and funding mechanism rather than specifying a binding standard or fee schedule, so it is treated as an enabling incentive/funding program.

policy_statement: Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings through measures to electrify existing buildings, standardize all-electric new development, and other decarbonization actions (including embodied carbon and refrigerants).
verbatim_text:
Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings
Measure E1: Transition Existing Buildings to All- Electric
Measure E2: Standardize All-Electric New Development
Measure E3: Other Decarbonization Actions
extraction_rationale: Named Strategy with multiple measures and actions underneath, forming a clear umbrella policy.
mechanism_description: The strategy transitions existing buildings to all-electric and standardizes all-electric new development to reduce building-related greenhouse gas emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy service provision (heat/cooking) → fuel switch (gas/oil → electricity) → ↓ on-site fossil combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human/system activity: Building owners/developers retrofit or construct buildings with electric end-use equipment (e.g., heat pumps, electric resistance where applicable, induction stoves) instead of gas/oil/propane systems; sometimes coupled with panel upgrades and wiring readiness.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of methane/oil in boilers/furnaces/water heaters and associated CO₂ (and some CH₄ leakage upstream) emissions; emissions may shift to centralized power generation, where they are typically lower per unit service and can decline over time with grid decarbonization.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in building-related GHG emissions (especially Scope 1), contributing to overall emissions mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is eliminating on-site fossil fuel combustion in buildings, thereby reducing direct GHG emissions (and often enabling further reductions as electricity supply decarbonizes).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Switching building end uses from fossil fuels to electricity reduces direct combustion emissions, which is a direct emissions mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While electrification can also improve energy efficiency (heat pumps) and local air quality, the mechanism as stated is fundamentally about reducing GHGs by eliminating on-site fossil fuel use; that is Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency (which would be framed as “same service with less energy input” irrespective of fuel).  
3) Edge cases applied: If the electricity supply is very carbon-intensive, near-term net GHG benefits can be smaller or delayed; nevertheless, the intent and primary mechanism remain emissions reduction via fuel switching, often paired with grid decarbonization assumptions.  
4) Primary applies to ALL instances: Any policy under “building electrification → building emissions reduction” shares the same core mitigation pathway—reducing fossil fuel combustion in buildings—regardless of whether it targets new builds, retrofits, incentives, or rate/program design.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy is code-based, incentive-based, public-sector procurement, or equity-focused (and whether it also materially increases efficiency or resilience) requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Net GHG impact can depend on grid emissions intensity and peak generation mix; some electrification measures may be pursued primarily for health/indoor air quality or cost reasons, but the canonical mechanism’s dominant climate effect remains mitigation via reduced on-site fossil combustion. In rare contexts with highly carbon-intensive grids and no decarbonization trajectory, short-run emissions could increase, but this does not change the mechanism’s primary classification.
key_indicators: “Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings”; “Transition Existing Buildings to All-Electric”; “Standardize All-Electric New Development”; “Other Decarbonization Actions”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements from reduced on-site combustion; potential energy cost stability; improved indoor air quality and safety (cooking); none explicitly stated in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: The measures could ultimately be implemented via mandates/standards (e.g., all-electric requirements), but the provided excerpt is only a strategy/measure listing without explicit regulatory language, funding, or compliance details, so classification remains planning-strategy.

policy_statement: Measure E2: Standardize all-electric new development by adopting an ordinance requiring applicable new buildings be fully electric (no natural gas hookups), adopting ZNE ordinances by specified years, and adopting CALGreen Tier 1 standards and potential Tier 2 amendments.
parent_statement: Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings through measures to electrify existing buildings, standardize all-electric new development, and other decarbonization actions (including embodied carbon and refrigerants).
verbatim_text:
Measure E2: Standardize All-Electric New Development
Energy: • Action E2.1 - Adopt an ordinance requiring all applicable new buildings to be fully electric with no natural gas hookups... • Action E2.2 - Adopt a ZNE ordinance for all new residential buildings built after 2025 and all new nonresidential buildings built after 2030... • Action E2.3 - Adopt CALGreen Code Tier 1 green building standards and identify which Tier 2 standards could be adopted as code amendments.
extraction_rationale: Measure contains clear regulatory commitments (ordinances, code adoption) and timelines.
mechanism_description: The city adopts ordinances and code standards requiring new buildings to be all-electric and meet zero net energy/CALGreen tiers, which reduces building energy use and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design/retrofit standards → ↓ delivered energy demand + ↓ on-site fossil fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Requirements for new construction and/or major renovations (and sometimes existing-building performance standards) that raise minimum efficiency, tighten envelopes, improve HVAC/lighting/water-heating performance, and may require electrification or low-carbon heat.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced heat transfer losses, improved system efficiencies (higher COP/AFUE equivalents), better controls and reduced peak loads; reduced combustion of natural gas/oil on-site; lower electricity demand from the grid.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower operational (Scope 1 and 2) energy-related GHG emissions over the building lifetime; in net-zero/energy-plus cases, near-zero or net-negative operational emissions depending on grid intensity and on-site generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building operational energy use (and fuel switching away from fossil combustion), which lowers GHG emissions from energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: These policies act on building energy performance so the largest effect is lowering operational energy consumption and fossil fuel use, which directly reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While efficiency also improves “resource efficiency,” the climate-dominant purpose and outcome here is emissions reduction from energy use; therefore Mitigation best matches the primary causal pathway.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a code is framed around resilience (e.g., passive survivability during outages/heatwaves) it could have an adaptation co-benefit, but the canonical mechanism explicitly targets energy/emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation primary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under “building_energy_code → energy_use_reduction” fundamentally reduces operational energy demand and/or on-site combustion, yielding emissions reductions regardless of city or code stringency.  
5) Note on subtypes: Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “efficiency,” “electrification,” “net-zero,” “performance standard”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and specific provisions.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some building-code policies can include adaptation-focused elements (cool roofs for heat, flood-proofing, backup power) or embodied-carbon limits; those would introduce additional pathways, but under this canonical mechanism the dominant pathway remains operational energy/emissions reduction (Mitigation).
key_indicators: "Adopt an ordinance requiring all applicable new buildings to be fully electric"; "no natural gas hookups"; "Adopt a ZNE ordinance"; "built after 2025... after 2030"; "Adopt CALGreen Code Tier 1"; "Tier 2 standards... adopted as code amendments"
co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality (reduced combustion byproducts), potential public health benefits, and possible energy bill savings for occupants.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although ZNE/CALGreen can be framed as “standards,” the text specifies adopting them via ordinance/code, making the instrument best classified as a regulatory mandate rather than an incentive or disclosure approach.
parent_policy_name: Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings through measures to electrify existing buildings, standardize all-electric new development, and other decarbonization actions (including embodied carbon and refrigerants).
rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the measure itself: it requires new buildings to be 'fully electric (no natural gas hookups)' and to 'Adopt a ZNE ordinance' for new residential/nonresidential buildings, and adopt higher 'CALGreen' tiers. Electrification and ZNE/building code tightening are standard Mitigation actions via reduced fossil fuel use and energy demand (consistent with canonical_mechanism building_energy_code → energy_use_reduction). The gate failure ('no Mitigation outcome token') is a tokenization issue; climate_screen is 'explicit_self', so keep.

co_benefits: Improved indoor air quality; reduced utility bills/energy cost burden; water savings; improved building resilience/comfort
instance_edge_case_notes: Tier 1 adoption is clearly a mandate; the “identify Tier 2 standards” portion is preparatory, but it is explicitly framed as potential future code amendments, keeping the overall instrument classification in the regulatory/mandate category.
parent_policy_name: Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings through measures to electrify existing buildings, standardize all-electric new development, and other decarbonization actions (including embodied carbon and refrigerants).
rescue_reasoning: 1) Climate relevance is explicit via the parent statement: "Strategy 5: Decarbonize Buildings..." which clearly indicates mitigation intent. 2) The policy action is a building-code standards upgrade: "Adopt CALGreen Code Tier 1 green building standards and identify which Tier 2 standards could be adopted as code amendments." This aligns with the canonical mechanism "building_energy_code → energy_use_reduction" because higher building standards commonly reduce building energy demand and related emissions. 3) The gate failure reason (no explicit mitigation outcome token) is outweighed by the explicit_parent climate screen and the decarbonization framing in the parent.

section_header: 2.6.2.2 Strategy 2: Increase Densities and Diversity of Land Uses near Transit
policy_statement: Strategy 2 will increase densities and diversity of land uses near transit to reduce vehicle miles traveled by coordinating land use development outcomes such as increased densities near transit, jobs-housing balance, and strategically located land uses.
verbatim_text:
## 2.6.2.2 Strategy 2: Increase Densities and Diversity of Land Uses near Transit

Strategy 2 focuses on coordinating land use development that leads to outcomes associated with reduced vehicle miles traveled, such as increased densities near transit, jobs-housing balance, and strategically located land uses that can reduce travel distances for many trip purposes.
extraction_rationale: Named strategy with defined intervention focus (land use coordination to reduce VMT); serves as umbrella for listed measures T1 and T2.
mechanism_description: Coordinate higher-density, mixed-use development near transit to shorten travel distances and reduce vehicle miles traveled, lowering transportation emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Urban form/land-use change → ↓ car dependence + ↓ VKT + ↑ mode shift → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Direct new development (especially jobs/non-residential and often mixed-use) into designated activity centers, corridors, and areas well-served by transit, creating denser, more connected, mixed-use neighborhoods.  
2) Mediating physical/social process: Shorter trip distances and improved access to transit and active modes reduce per-capita driving and increase the share of trips by lower-carbon modes; over time, this also reduces the need for parking and road expansion that would otherwise induce additional driving.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower gasoline/diesel consumption and associated CO₂ (and co-pollutant) emissions from on-road transportation; in some cases, additional mitigation occurs via reduced greenfield conversion and more efficient infrastructure/service delivery.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing transport-sector GHG emissions by lowering car travel (VKT) and enabling mode shift through compact, transit-served land-use patterns.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Land-use concentration around transit and amenities reduces VKT and increases mode share of transit/active travel, cutting fuel combustion emissions—this is a direct GHG mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While compact growth can also improve resource efficiency (e.g., shorter utility networks) and can support adaptation outcomes (e.g., reduced heat exposure if designed well), the mechanism as stated and commonly used is principally to reduce transport emissions via travel behavior and mode shift.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some “activity center” policies are framed around livability or access (e.g., 10-minute city) and may not explicitly mention emissions; however, the dominant climate-relevant effect across such policies remains VKT reduction and mode shift, which is mitigation.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is “transit-oriented/compact development concentrating growth in centers/corridors” operates through the same core pathway of reduced car dependence and travel emissions, so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy also delivers meaningful adaptation (heat, flood risk) or nature-based co-benefits depends on design details (greenspace, building standards, hazard siting), which must be evaluated at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “10-minute city” or activity-center policy is implemented primarily through greening/cooling interventions or hazard-avoidance siting (rather than travel reduction), adaptation or nature-based pathways could be prominent; nonetheless, for the specified mechanism (TOD/compact growth to reduce car travel), mitigation remains dominant.
key_indicators: “Strategy 2: Increase Densities and Diversity of Land Uses near Transit”; “coordinating land use development”; “reduced vehicle miles traveled”; “increased densities near transit”; “jobs-housing balance”; “reduce travel distances”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced congestion, improved air quality, improved transit viability/accessibility, health benefits from more walking/transit use, reduced infrastructure/parking demand.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be implemented via regulatory upzoning/TOD requirements, but the excerpt itself is framed as a strategy (planning) without specifying mandates, standards, or incentives.

section_header: Measure T1: Increase Density near High-Quality Transit Areas.
policy_statement: Measure T1 will increase density near High-Quality Transit Areas (HQTAs) by setting minimum and maximum dwelling unit density ranges, locating most residential and employment centers within 1 mile of an HQTA, and increasing dwelling units within HQTAs.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 will increase densities and diversity of land uses near transit to reduce vehicle miles traveled by coordinating land use development outcomes such as increased densities near transit, jobs-housing balance, and strategically located land uses.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T1: Increase Density near High-Quality Transit Areas.

The performance objectives for Measure T1 are to: (1) achieve a minimum of 20 dwelling units (DUs) per acre (maximum of 30-150 DUs per acre) for HQTAs; (2) locate a majority of residential and employment centers in the unincorporated Los Angeles County within 1 mile of an HQTA; and (3) achieve a 27 percent increase in dwelling units within HQTAs.
extraction_rationale: Specific measure with quantified performance objectives for density and location, linked under Strategy 2.
mechanism_description: The measure sets density objectives and concentrates housing and jobs near high-quality transit areas to support more transit use and less driving, reducing transportation emissions and associated land-use impacts.

section_header: Measure T2: Develop Land Use Plans Addressing Jobs-Housing Balance and Increase Mixed Use.
policy_statement: Measure T2 will develop land use plans to address jobs-housing balance and increase mixed use, including achieving job density targets for new nonresidential projects and requiring community plans to identify strategies to reduce jobs/housing imbalance below 20%.
parent_statement: Strategy 2 will increase densities and diversity of land uses near transit to reduce vehicle miles traveled by coordinating land use development outcomes such as increased densities near transit, jobs-housing balance, and strategically located land uses.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T2: Develop Land Use Plans Addressing Jobs-Housing Balance and Increase Mixed Use.

The performance objective for Measure T2 is to achieve a job density of 300 jobs per acre for all new projects with nonresidential development by 2030. For communities with an imbalance of jobs/housing (±20 percent), community plans will identify and quantify strategies for bringing that imbalance below 20 percent.
extraction_rationale: Quantified target and planning requirement stated as a measure under Strategy 2.
mechanism_description: Developing land use and community plans to increase mixed use and improve jobs–housing balance reduces commute distances and vehicle miles traveled, which lowers transportation emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Land-use pattern (jobs–housing balance + mixed use) → ↓ travel distance/auto dependence → ↓ VMT and fuel use → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes the built environment by planning/zoning for mixed-use development and improved proximity between housing and employment (and often higher job density in appropriate areas). (2) This alters travel behavior through shorter required trip distances, improved feasibility of walking/transit, and reduced reliance on private vehicles, leading to reduced vehicle activity and fuel consumption. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower CO₂ (and other) emissions from on-road transportation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions from fewer/shorter vehicle trips (lower VMT and fuel combustion).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: land-use planning shifts trip patterns → reduced VMT/fuel use → reduced transportation GHG emissions.  
2) Primary category choice: this is direct emissions reduction (Mitigation), not primarily resource efficiency (though it can co-deliver energy/material savings) and not adaptation/NBS as its main mechanism is not hazard reduction or ecosystem carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases: if a plan is framed mainly for resilience (e.g., evacuation, wildfire risk avoidance) it could be adaptation, but that is not inherent to this canonical mechanism; similarly, if the dominant claim were energy savings in buildings, that would be a different mechanism.  
4) Applicability to all instances: any policy whose core mechanism is improving jobs–housing balance and mixed use to reduce commute distance/VMT will have its primary climate effect through transportation emissions reductions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., mode shift vs. trip reduction, equity co-benefits) is deferred to policy-level evidence and is not determined at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be partially confounded by induced demand or rebound effects, and outcomes depend on complementary transit/parking/pricing policies; however, the intended dominant pathway remains transportation emissions mitigation via reduced VMT.
key_indicators: “performance objective”; “achieve a job density of 300 jobs per acre”; “by 2030”; “community plans will identify and quantify strategies”; “bringing that imbalance below 20 percent”
co_benefits: Reduced congestion and travel time; improved accessibility; potential air quality improvements
instance_edge_case_notes: Although implemented through “community plans” (a planning vehicle), the presence of explicit quantitative thresholds and a deadline makes “performance-standard” the best fit over a general planning-strategy classification.
parent_policy_name: Strategy 2 will increase densities and diversity of land uses near transit to reduce vehicle miles traveled by coordinating land use development outcomes such as increased densities near transit, jobs-housing balance, and strategically located land uses.
rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the parent statement: it will 'increase densities and diversity of land uses near transit to reduce vehicle miles traveled.' The measure text supports that mechanism by committing to 'develop land use plans to address jobs-housing balance and increase mixed use' and setting a 'job density of 300 jobs per acre' plus strategies to bring 'jobs/housing imbalance below 20 percent.' These are land-use planning actions that implement the parent’s VMT-reduction mitigation pathway, so the row should be retained as Mitigation despite the lack of an explicit mitigation token in the sub-measure text.

section_header: 2.6.2.3 Strategy 3: Reduce Single-Occupancy Vehicle Trips
policy_statement: Strategy 3 will reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips by developing transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes.
verbatim_text:
## 2.6.2.3 Strategy 3: Reduce Single-Occupancy Vehicle Trips

Strategy 3 focuses on development of transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes to help reduce trips made in single-occupancy vehicles.
extraction_rationale: Named strategy functioning as umbrella for measures T3–T5.
mechanism_description: Develop transportation networks that make active travel more accessible and convenient to reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips and cut transport emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Mobility system change (↑ transit access + ↑ multimodal connectivity) → modal shift (↓ single-occupancy driving, ↓ VKT) → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Cities invest in and redesign mobility access (mobility hubs, park-and-ride, bike/scooter links, integrated transfers) to make transit and shared/active modes easier and more reliable than driving alone.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced internal-combustion vehicle use lowers gasoline/diesel combustion (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions); in some cases, congestion relief can further reduce per-km emissions, though rebound can offset.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transport CO₂e emissions through lower vehicle travel and substitution toward lower-emitting passenger-km modes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced tailpipe (and upstream) GHG emissions from fewer car trips and/or shorter car travel due to a modal shift to lower-carbon transport.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Improve multimodal connectivity to enable modal shift away from single-occupancy vehicles, leading to lower fuel combustion and lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such hubs can improve accessibility (a co-benefit) and sometimes reduce energy use per trip (resource efficiency), the defining and largest climate effect across policies sharing this mechanism is emissions mitigation via reduced car use.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “mobility hub” primarily induces additional driving (e.g., large park-and-ride generating new trips) or is implemented mainly for resilience/evacuation, the dominant effect could weaken or shift; however, the stated mechanism explicitly targets modal shift to sustainable transport and emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is shifting trips from single-occupancy driving to transit/active/shared modes will primarily operate through transport emissions reductions, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy’s realized impact is large, and whether it also qualifies for secondary labels (e.g., air quality, equity, congestion, adaptation co-benefits), depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Park-and-ride elements can create induced driving or shift trips from active modes to car-to-transit, reducing net benefits; outcomes depend on parking supply/pricing, transit frequency, land-use context, and whether buses/rail are low-carbon. If transit vehicles are diesel and poorly utilized, emissions reductions may be smaller, but the intended dominant pathway remains mitigation via modal shift.
key_indicators: "reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips"; "development of transportation networks"; "increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes"
co_benefits: Improved air quality; reduced congestion; public health benefits from walking/cycling; traffic safety improvements; equity/accessibility improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy if the underlying action is primarily network planning rather than capital build-out, but the wording "development of transportation networks" most strongly signals infrastructure investment.

section_header: Measure T3: Expand Bicycle and Pedestrian Network to Serve Residential, Employment, and Recreational Trips.
policy_statement: Measure T3 will expand the bicycle and pedestrian network, including increasing bikeway miles by 300% by 2035 and implementing the County Bicycle Master Plan and updating pedestrian/bicycle/active transportation plans every five years.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 will reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips by developing transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T3: Expand Bicycle and Pedestrian Network to Serve Residential, Employment, and Recreational Trips.

The performance objective for Measure T3 is to increase bikeway miles by 300 percent by 2035. This measure also requires implementing the County Bicycle Master Plan and completing updates to County's Pedestrian Action Plan, Bicycle Master Plan, and Active Transportation Plans every five years.
extraction_rationale: Quantified target (300% by 2035) and specific implementation requirements; listed under Strategy 3.
mechanism_description: The measure expands bicycle and pedestrian networks and implements/updates active transportation plans to support more trips by biking and walking, reducing vehicle travel and transportation emissions.

2) Mediating physical process: Reduced internal-combustion vehicle use lowers gasoline/diesel combustion (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions); in some cases, congestion relief can further reduce per-km emissions, though rebound can offset.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transport CO₂e emissions through lower vehicle travel and substitution toward lower-emitting passenger-km modes.

4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is shifting trips from single-occupancy driving to transit/active/shared modes will primarily operate through transport emissions reductions, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy’s realized impact is large, and whether it also qualifies for secondary labels (e.g., air quality, equity, congestion, adaptation co-benefits), depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.

section_header: Measure T4: Broaden Options for Transit, Active Transportation, and Alternative Modes of Transportation.
policy_statement: Measure T4 will broaden options for transit and alternative modes by 2030 by doubling transit service hours, installing bus-only lanes and signal prioritization on major transit thoroughfares, and ensuring at least 75% of unincorporated County residents live within one-half mile of a bus or active transportation option.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 will reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips by developing transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T4: Broaden Options for Transit, Active Transportation, and Alternative Modes of Transportation.

The performance objectives for Measure T4 are by 2030 to: (1) double transit service hours from 560,000 to 1.12 million; (2) install bus-only lanes and signal prioritization on all major transit thoroughfares; and (3) have a minimum of 75 percent of unincorporated Los Angeles County residents live within one-half mile of a bus or active transportation option;
extraction_rationale: Contains concrete, quantified performance objectives and infrastructure actions; listed under Strategy 3.
mechanism_description: The measure will expand and prioritize bus and other transit/active transportation options, improving access and service so more trips shift away from driving and transportation emissions decrease.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Transport demand + mode choice → ↑ public transit use / ↓ private vehicle VKT → ↓ fuel combustion (or ↓ grid electricity per passenger-km vs cars) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Cities expand, modernize, and increase the attractiveness/capacity/reliability of public transport (service frequency, coverage, speed, fleet quality), leading travelers to substitute some car trips with transit.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Fewer car trips reduce gasoline/diesel combustion; transit moves more passengers per vehicle, typically lowering energy use per passenger-km, and can further reduce stop-and-go driving through less congestion.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂e emissions from the transport sector (tailpipe emissions and, depending on fleet powertrain and grid mix, upstream energy emissions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from on-road transport due to mode shift from private car travel to public transit (and related reductions in per-passenger energy use).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: transit service expansion increases transit modal share, displacing private vehicle travel and reducing fossil fuel combustion emissions—this is a direct GHG mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although transit can have resource-efficiency co-benefits (moving people with less energy per passenger-km) and air-quality/health benefits, the principal climate-relevant endpoint is still emissions reduction (mitigation), not adaptation or nature-based outcomes.  
3) Edge cases applied: If added transit service does not induce meaningful mode shift (e.g., mostly induced demand or operational emissions dominate), mitigation impact could be smaller; however, the mechanism as defined assumes/supports mode shift and emissions reduction as the main effect. Electrification of transit fleets is a related but distinct mechanism; it can amplify mitigation but is not required for this classification.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances: Across Austin (Project Connect), Budapest (increase modal share via modernization), and Chicago (ridership increase target), the shared mechanism is expanding/improving transit to reduce transport emissions via mode shift—consistently mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy is chiefly infrastructure investment vs operations, includes electrification, or bundles demand-management measures requires policy-specific evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “transit expansion” policy is primarily framed/implemented for climate resilience (e.g., hardening assets against floods/heat) without targeting mode shift, it would be an adaptation mechanism instead; if expansion increases service but raises net emissions due to low ridership and high-emitting operations, realized mitigation may be limited though the canonical mechanism remains mitigation-oriented.
key_indicators: “performance objectives … by 2030”; “double transit service hours”; “install bus-only lanes and signal prioritization”; “minimum of 75 percent … live within one-half mile of a bus or active transportation option”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollution; reduced traffic congestion; improved road safety; increased physical activity/public health; improved transportation equity and access
instance_edge_case_notes: This could also be classified as infrastructure-investment because it includes capital actions (bus-only lanes, signal prioritization) and service expansion; however, the explicit quantified “performance objectives … by 2030” makes target-setting the primary instrument framing.
parent_policy_name: Strategy 3 will reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips by developing transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes.

section_header: Measure T5: Limit and Remove Parking Minimums.
policy_statement: Measure T5 will limit and remove parking minimums through parking supply reductions, unbundling parking costs, and implementing parking pricing to encourage park-once behavior.
parent_statement: Strategy 3 will reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips by developing transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T5: Limit and Remove Parking Minimums.

The performance objectives for Measure T5 are to: (1) reduce parking stipulations to reduce parking supply and encourage transit use; (2) unbundle parking costs to reflect the cost of parking; and (3) implement parking pricing to encourage 'park-once' behavior.
extraction_rationale: Specific intervention set (parking reforms) framed as performance objectives; listed under Strategy 3.
mechanism_description: The measure reduces parking supply, unbundles parking costs, and implements parking pricing to encourage transit use and park-once behavior, which reduces driving and related transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Parking policy reform → ↑ cost/↓ availability of parking → ↓ driving (↓ VMT, ↓ cruising) + mode shift → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Cities remove/limit parking minimums, reduce parking supply, unbundle parking from housing/retail costs, and/or price curb/garage parking to reflect demand.  
2) Mediating physical process: Higher monetary/time costs and reduced convenience of storing a private car decreases car ownership/use, reduces discretionary trips, and reduces cruising/idling while searching for parking; some trips shift to lower-carbon modes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower vehicle fuel consumption leads to reduced tailpipe CO₂e emissions (and often reduced congestion-related emissions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing VMT and idling/cruising, which lowers gasoline/diesel combustion and thus transport GHG emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Parking supply/pricing reform changes travel behavior → reduces VMT/cruising → reduces fuel combustion → reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although reducing parking can improve space efficiency (Resource Efficiency) or enable land-use changes, the core mechanism described and shared across policies is behavior-driven reduction in driving emissions, which is direct Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific policy’s main intent/effect is land redevelopment (e.g., housing infill) or streetscape cooling/flood resilience, those are co-benefits; however, they are not the dominant, necessary effect of “parking pricing/supply reform” as a mechanism.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that reduces subsidized/abundant parking via pricing/unbundling/supply cuts aims to deter driving and therefore primarily mitigates by cutting transport emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further labeling (e.g., “pricing instrument,” “demand management,” “TOD/land-use synergy”) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: In some contexts, converting parking to housing or mixed-use could make land-use change the larger long-run mitigation driver; conversely, if pricing causes spillover cruising to adjacent areas without complementary management, near-term emission benefits may be reduced. These do not alter the primary classification for the canonical mechanism.
key_indicators: “Limit and Remove Parking Minimums”; “reduce parking stipulations”; “reduce parking supply”; “unbundle parking costs”; “implement parking pricing”; “encourage transit use”; “park-once”
co_benefits: Reduced congestion/cruising, improved local air quality, reduced noise, improved walkability/streetscape potential, housing affordability impacts via unbundling (potential)
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes a clear pricing component (“implement parking pricing”) which could justify a secondary classification as a pricing-mechanism, but the headline action is removal/limitation of parking minimums (regulatory reform), so regulatory-mandate is selected as primary instrument type.
parent_policy_name: Strategy 3 will reduce single-occupancy vehicle trips by developing transportation networks that increase the accessibility, comfort, and convenience of active travel modes.

section_header: 2.6.2.4 Strategy 4: Institutionalize Low-Carbon Transportation
policy_statement: Strategy 4 will institutionalize low-carbon transportation by increasing access to charging infrastructure, installing EV charging stations, requiring new development to install EV charging, and developing incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EV charging stations.
verbatim_text:
## 2.6.2.4 Strategy 4: Institutionalize Low-Carbon Transportation

The County also would endeavor to install EV charging stations (EVCSs) at County properties and in the public right-of-way, require new development to install EVCSs, and develop incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EVCSs.
extraction_rationale: Named strategy with explicit interventions; umbrella for measures T6–T9.
mechanism_description: The County would install EV charging stations at County properties and in the public right-of-way and require new development to install EV charging, enabling wider EV adoption.
primary_causal_pathway: Charging access expansion → ↑ EV adoption/EV miles → ↓ fossil fuel combustion in transport → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Human activity/system changed: The city deploys and/or enables a distributed network of EV chargers (Level 1/2/DC fast), improving access (including equity-focused siting) and reducing “range anxiety” and charging inconvenience.  
2) Mediating physical process: Improved charging availability increases EV purchase and utilization, displacing gasoline/diesel combustion; energy demand shifts from liquid fuels to electricity.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transport-sector GHG emissions (direct tailpipe CO₂ is avoided; upstream emissions depend on electricity generation and charging demand patterns).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing transportation GHG emissions by enabling substitution of internal-combustion vehicle miles traveled with electric vehicle miles.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Expanding charging infrastructure primarily functions as an enabling measure for EV adoption and use, which reduces fossil fuel combustion in road transport and therefore reduces GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While equity, air quality, and grid-management co-benefits may be important, the principal climate-relevant lever shared by all such policies is emissions reduction via electrification of vehicles, i.e., mitigation rather than adaptation/resource efficiency/nature-based solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is high-carbon, near-term lifecycle benefits may be smaller, but the mechanism still targets transport decarbonization through electrification; any grid decarbonization interactions are secondary and not required for the mechanism to be mitigation-oriented.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy whose core mechanism is “charging infrastructure → EV adoption enabling” aims to increase EV uptake/use; the consistent dominant climate effect is lower transport emissions from reduced liquid-fuel combustion.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy is more focused on public vs private charging, workplace charging, equity siting, managed charging/V2G, or complementary incentives is determined at the individual policy level and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If charging is deployed primarily for resilience/emergency power (e.g., V2G hubs) the dominant pathway could shift toward adaptation, but that is a different mechanism than general EV adoption enabling; rebound effects (more VMT due to lower operating costs) can partially offset reductions but typically do not change the primary mitigation classification.
key_indicators: “install EV charging stations (EVCSs) at County properties”; “in the public right-of-way”; “require new development to install EVCSs”; “develop incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EVCSs”; “Institutionalize Low-Carbon Transportation”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality and public health from reduced tailpipe pollution; potential transportation equity and access benefits depending on siting; reduced noise in some corridors.
instance_edge_case_notes: The statement bundles several instrument types (infrastructure investment + regulatory mandate for new development + incentive program/requirements for existing buildings). Classified to the most central/explicit action (public charger installation), but implementation likely spans multiple instruments.

section_header: Measure T6: Increase ZEV Market Share and Reduce Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Sales.
policy_statement: Measure T6 will increase zero-emission vehicle (ZEV) market share and EV charging stations, including targets for fleetwide ZEV penetration, ZEV sales shares, total new EV charging stations, and EV charging stations at County facilities by 2030/2035/2045.
parent_statement: Strategy 4 will institutionalize low-carbon transportation by increasing access to charging infrastructure, installing EV charging stations, requiring new development to install EV charging, and developing incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EV charging stations.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T6: Increase ZEV Market Share and Reduce Gasoline and Diesel Fuel Sales.

The performance objectives for Measure T6 are to: (1) increase the fleetwide percentage of light-duty vehicles in the unincorporated Los Angeles County that are ZEVs to 30 percent by 2030, 50 percent by 2035, and 90 percent by 2045; (2) increase the sales of new light-duty vehicles in the unincorporated Los Angeles County that are ZEVs to 68 percent by 2030 and 100 percent by 2035; (3) install 37,000 total new public and private shared EVCSs (including EVCSs at County facilities and properties) by 2030, 74,000 by 2035, and 140,000 by 2045; and (4) install 5,000 total new EVCSs at County facilities and properties by 2030, 10,000 by 2035, and 25,000 by 2045.
extraction_rationale: Multiple quantified targets with timelines; listed as a measure under Strategy 4.
mechanism_description: The measure sets objectives to expand EV charging stations and increase ZEV fleet and sales shares, enabling wider EV adoption and reducing gasoline and diesel vehicle emissions.

section_header: Measure T7: Electrify County Fleet Vehicles.
policy_statement: Measure T7 will electrify County fleet vehicles by electrifying bus and shuttle fleets by 2035, increasing County-owned light-duty ZEV shares by 2030/2035/2045, and supporting the state's goal that new light-duty fleet purchases (with exceptions) will be ZEVs.
parent_statement: Strategy 4 will institutionalize low-carbon transportation by increasing access to charging infrastructure, installing EV charging stations, requiring new development to install EV charging, and developing incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EV charging stations.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T7: Electrify County Fleet Vehicles.

The performance objectives for Measure T7 are to: (1) electrify the County bus and shuttle fleets by 2035; (2) increase the fleetwide percentage of light-duty vehicles in the Countyowned fleet that are ZEVs to 35 percent by 2030, 60 percent by 2035, and 100 percent by 2045; and (3) support the state's goal that all new light-duty vehicle fleet purchases, with certain exceptions, will be ZEVs.
extraction_rationale: Concrete electrification commitments and quantified targets; under Strategy 4.
mechanism_description: The County plans to electrify its bus, shuttle, and light-duty fleets by increasing the share of zero-emission vehicles, reducing tailpipe transportation emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Transport activity (VMT) shift: ICE → EV → ↓ fossil fuel combustion (tailpipe) + potentially ↓ lifecycle CO₂e → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: The vehicle fleet and miles traveled shift from internal combustion engine (ICE) cars/buses/trucks to battery-electric (and sometimes plug-in hybrid) vehicles, including public fleets and private ownership.  
2) Mediating process: EV drivetrains eliminate tailpipe CO₂ from gasoline/diesel combustion and can reduce total energy per mile; remaining emissions depend on electricity generation and upstream fuel/vehicle lifecycle.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂e emissions per mile and reduced total transport-sector emissions, especially as the electricity grid decarbonizes.
dominant_pathway_test: Replacing internal-combustion vehicle miles with electric vehicle miles most strongly reduces GHG emissions from fuel combustion (and, depending on grid mix, upstream energy supply), lowering net transportation-sector emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Substitution of ICE travel with EV travel reduces fossil fuel combustion and associated CO₂e emissions—this is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although EVs can improve energy efficiency (a resource-efficiency co-benefit) and reduce air pollution/health burdens, the principal climate-relevant effect is emissions reduction rather than hazard reduction (adaptation) or ecosystem carbon/heat services (nature-based).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy focused only on electrification for resilience (e.g., backup power for emergency response fleets) without an emissions objective, classification might differ; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly links fleet electrification to transport emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation dominant. Grid carbon intensity affects magnitude but not the direction of the primary pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is “fleet electrification → transport emissions reduction” fundamentally operates by reducing CO₂e from combustion per mile and/or total combustion miles, which is mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further subtype (e.g., public fleet vs private adoption; infrastructure vs mandates; equity-oriented design) requires policy-specific evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented mainly via promoting hybrids (non-plug-in) or if electricity is extremely high-carbon, net GHG benefits can be smaller; nonetheless the mechanism’s dominant intent/effect remains mitigation through reduced combustion-related emissions. If a policy bundles electrification with VMT growth, rebound could partially offset reductions—this affects impact size, not primary category.
key_indicators: “performance objectives”; “electrify the County bus and shuttle fleets by 2035”; “increase…percentage…that are ZEVs to 35 percent by 2030, 60 percent by 2035, and 100 percent by 2045”; “support the state's goal that all new light-duty vehicle fleet purchases…will be ZEVs”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (NOx/PM) and potential public health benefits; reduced noise; potential fuel/maintenance cost savings
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a procurement requirement/mandate because it concerns fleet purchases, but the text is framed as “performance objectives” with percentage milestones rather than a binding purchasing rule; classified as target-setting accordingly.
parent_policy_name: Strategy 4 will institutionalize low-carbon transportation by increasing access to charging infrastructure, installing EV charging stations, requiring new development to install EV charging, and developing incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EV charging stations.

section_header: Measure T8: Accelerate Freight Decarbonization.
policy_statement: Measure T8 will accelerate freight decarbonization with ZEV targets for medium- and heavy-duty fleets and drayage, ZEV sales targets, and requirements for EV charging stations at new and existing warehouse loading docks by 2030.
parent_statement: Strategy 4 will institutionalize low-carbon transportation by increasing access to charging infrastructure, installing EV charging stations, requiring new development to install EV charging, and developing incentives and requirements for existing buildings to install EV charging stations.
verbatim_text:
## Measure T8: Accelerate Freight Decarbonization.

The performance objectives for Measure T8 are to: (1) increase the fleetwide percentage of medium-duty and heavy-duty vehicles in the unincorporated Los Angeles County that are ZEVs to 40 percent by 2030, 60 percent by 2035, and 90 percent by 2045; (2) increase the fleetwide percentage of medium-duty and heavy-duty trucks in the County-owned fleet that are ZEVs to 50 percent by 2030, 70 percent by 2035, and 95 percent by 2045; (3) ensure that 100 percent of the drayage truck fleet is ZEV by 2035; (4) ensure that 100 percent of sales of medium- and heavy-duty trucks are ZEVs by 2045; (5) require that all new warehouse loading docks have EVCSs by 2030; and (6) require that all existing warehouse loading docks have EVCSs by 2030.
extraction_rationale: Quantified ZEV targets and explicit charging infrastructure requirements; measure under Strategy 4.
mechanism_description: The measure increases adoption of zero-emission medium- and heavy-duty and drayage trucks, reducing freight transport tailpipe emissions, and it requires EV charging stations at warehouse loading docks to support ZEV operation.

section_header: Measure T9: Expand Use of Zero-Emission Technologies for Off-Road Vehicles and Equipment.
policy_statement: Measure T9 will expand use of zero-emission off-road vehicles and equipment by increasing ZEV shares for off-road fleets and for construction/agriculture/manufacturing equipment by 2030/2035/2045.

verbatim_text:
## Measure T9: Expand Use of Zero-Emission Technologies for Off-Road Vehicles and Equipment.

The performance objectives for Measure T9 are to: (1) increase the fleetwide percentage of off-road fleet and equipment in the unincorporated Los Angeles County that are ZEVs to 20 percent by 2030, 50 percent by 2035, and 95 percent by 2045; and (2) increase the fleetwide percentage of construction, agriculture, and manufacturing equipment in the unincorporated Los Angeles County that are ZEVs to 50 percent by 2030, 75 percent by 2035, and 100 percent by 2045.
extraction_rationale: Specific quantified targets for off-road equipment electrification/zero-emission transition; under Strategy 4.
mechanism_description: The measure increases the share of zero-emission off-road vehicles and equipment, which reduces combustion fuel use and associated transportation emissions.
